condemn.definition: to officially declare a building unsafe to live in
condemn.example: The council condemned the old warehouse after the roof collapsed.

demolish.definition: to knock down or destroy a building
demolish.example: They demolished the factory to make space for new apartments.

develop.definition: to build or improve land or property
develop.example: The company plans to develop the empty lot into a shopping centre.

devise.definition: to invent or create a plan, method, or device
devise.example: The architect devised a clever cooling system for the house.

maintain.definition: to keep something in good condition
maintain.example: Tenants must maintain the property to avoid extra fees.

occupy.definition: to live in or use a space
occupy.example: No one has occupied the building since it was declared unsafe.

reconstruct.definition: to build something again after it has been damaged
reconstruct.example: The team reconstructed the bridge using stronger materials.

renovate.definition: to repair and improve a building to make it new again
renovate.example: They renovated the apartment before moving in.

structure.definition: something that has been built, such as a building or bridge
structure.example: The structure was strong enough to support heavy loads.

frame.definition: the supporting skeleton of a building
frame.example: The steel frame allowed the skyscraper to reach new heights.

construction.example: Construction of the new stadium will take two years.

timber.example: The cottage was built entirely from local timber.

steel.definition: a strong metal used in modern construction
steel.example: Steel beams support the weight of the high-rise building.

hoist.definition: to lift something heavy, usually with ropes or machinery
hoist.example: Workers hoisted the platform to the upper level.

hauling.example: The system was designed for hauling goods between floors.

lift shaft.definition: the vertical passage in which an elevator moves
lift shaft.example: The engineers inspected the lift shaft for safety issues.

trigger.definition: to cause something to start or activate
trigger.example: A slack rope would trigger the safety mechanism.

device.definition: a piece of equipment designed for a specific purpose
device.example: Otis invented a device that prevented elevators from falling.

internal.example: The building’s internal frame supported the entire structure.

landmark.definition: a building or structure that is easily recognisable
landmark.example: Skyscrapers often become landmarks in major cities.

innovation.definition: a new idea, method, or invention
innovation.example: The team won an award for their innovation in bridge design.

innovative.example: They used an innovative approach to avoid nails or screws.

engineer.definition: a person who designs or builds machines, structures, or systems
engineer.example: The engineer tested the model to ensure it was safe.

design.definition: to plan or create; the plan or drawing of something
design.example: The students designed a bridge using only timber.

foundation.definition: the solid base on which a building is constructed
foundation.example: The foundation must be strong to support the entire structure.

insulation.definition: material used to prevent heat, sound, or electricity from escaping
insulation.example: Good insulation keeps the house warm in winter.

reinforce.definition: to strengthen a structure using additional materials
reinforce.example: The engineers reinforced the walls with steel bars.

assemble.definition: to put parts together to form a structure
assemble.example: Workers assembled the prefabricated panels on-site.

prefabricated.definition: built in sections in a factory and transported for assembly
prefabricated.example: Prefabricated homes can be built much faster than traditional ones.

sustainable.example: Sustainable materials reduce the building’s carbon footprint.

load-bearing.definition: supporting the weight of a structure
load-bearing.example: The load-bearing walls cannot be removed without reinforcement.

concrete.definition: a building material made from cement, sand, and gravel
concrete.example: The bridge pillars were made of reinforced concrete.

brickwork.definition: the construction or pattern of bricks in a wall
brickwork.example: The brickwork on the old house was beautifully detailed.

mortar.definition: a mixture used to bind bricks or stones
mortar.example: The mason applied fresh mortar between the stones.

plaster.definition: a material used to coat walls and ceilings
plaster.example: The ceiling was finished with smooth plaster.

glazing.definition: glass installed in windows or doors

paneling.definition: wooden or decorative panels covering a wall
paneling.example: The living room featured elegant wooden paneling.

aesthetic.definition: related to beauty or artistic style
aesthetic.example: The building’s aesthetic appeal comes from its clean lines.

ergonomic.example: The ergonomic layout of the kitchen makes cooking easier.

blueprint.definition: a detailed technical drawing of a building
blueprint.example: The architect reviewed the blueprint before construction began.

façade.definition: the front or exterior face of a building
façade.example: The glass façade reflects the surrounding skyline.

layout.definition: the arrangement of rooms or elements in a space
layout.example: The open-plan layout creates a spacious feel.

ventilation.definition: the movement of air through a building

mechanism.definition: a system of parts working together in a machine
mechanism.example: The locking mechanism failed due to rust.

counterweight.definition: a weight that balances another weight
counterweight.example: Elevators use counterweights to reduce motor strain.

hydraulic.definition: operated by the pressure of a fluid
hydraulic.example: Hydraulic lifts are common in low-rise buildings.

automation.definition: the use of machines to perform tasks automatically
automation.example: Home automation systems control lighting and heating.

sensor.definition: a device that detects changes in the environment
sensor.example: Motion sensors turn on the lights automatically.

appliance.definition: a machine used in the home

smart home.definition: a home equipped with automated digital systems
smart home.example: The smart home adjusts the temperature automatically.

energy-efficient.definition: using less energy to perform the same function

gadget.example: The house of the future was filled with gadgets.

scaffolding.example: Workers stood on scaffolding to repair the roof.

crane.definition: a tall machine used for lifting heavy objects
crane.example: The crane lifted steel beams into place.

welding.definition: joining metal parts by heating them
welding.example: Welding is essential in steel-frame construction.

prototype.definition: the first working example of a machine or device
prototype.example: The prototype was tested before mass production began.

virtual.example: Virtual reality allows users to explore digital worlds.

state-of-the-art.definition: very modern; using the latest technology
state-of-the-art.example: The lab is equipped with state-of-the-art computers.

exceed.example: Sales exceeded all expectations last quarter.

digital.definition: relating to computers or electronic technology
digital.example: Digital devices are now part of everyday life.

screen.definition: a surface on which images or data are displayed
screen.example: The screen went black during the presentation.

computerised.example: The system is fully computerised and requires no manual input.

automated.definition: operated automatically by machines or computers
automated.example: The warehouse uses an automated sorting system.

remote control.definition: a device used to operate machines from a distance
remote control.example: She used the remote control to change the channel.

wireless connection.definition: a network connection without physical cables
wireless connection.example: My phone connects to the internet via a wireless connection.

labour-saving.definition: designed to reduce the amount of work needed
labour-saving.example: The dishwasher is a great labour-saving device.

cyberspace.definition: the virtual environment of the internet
cyberspace.example: Many people store their data in cyberspace.

cyber addiction.definition: excessive use of digital devices or the internet
cyber addiction.example: Cyber addiction is becoming a concern among teenagers.

silicon chip.definition: a small piece of silicon used in electronics to store data
silicon chip.example: The silicon chip made computers smaller and faster.

scroll.definition: to move up or down on a screen
scroll.example: Scroll down to read the rest of the article.

keyboard.definition: a set of keys used to input data into a computer
keyboard.example: She typed the report using a wireless keyboard.

function.definition: the purpose or role of something
function.example: Each button has a different function.

display.definition: to show something on a screen
display.example: The monitor displays the system status.

feature.definition: a distinctive attribute or aspect of something
feature.example: The phone’s best feature is its camera.

store.definition: to save or keep data or items
store.example: You can store files on the cloud.

data.example: The app collects user data for analysis.

processor.definition: the part of a computer that performs calculations
processor.example: This laptop has a fast processor for gaming.

technological era.definition: a period defined by rapid technological development
technological era.example: We are living in a technological era of constant innovation.

digital era.definition: a time when digital technology dominates daily life
digital era.example: The digital era has transformed how we communicate.

computer program.definition: a set of instructions that a computer follows
computer program.example: He wrote a computer program to sort the data.

television programme.definition: a scheduled show broadcast on TV
television programme.example: My favourite television programme airs on Friday nights.

flora.definition: plants of a particular region or time
flora.example: The flora of Australia includes many unique species.

fauna.definition: animals of a particular region or time
fauna.example: The fauna of Africa includes lions and elephants.

habitat.definition: the natural home of a plant or animal
habitat.example: Meerkats live in dry, open habitats.

predator.definition: an animal that hunts and eats other animals
predator.example: The lion is a predator at the top of the food chain.

natural balance.definition: the equilibrium between organisms and their environment
natural balance.example: Pollution can disrupt the natural balance of ecosystems.

introduced species.definition: a species brought to a new area by humans
introduced species.example: The cane toad is an introduced species in Australia.

genetically modified.definition: having genes altered by scientific methods
genetically modified.example: Genetically modified crops resist pests better than natural ones.

ecological balance.definition: the stable relationship between living organisms and their environment

extinct.example: Many species have become extinct due to habitat loss.

vulnerable.definition: at risk of harm or extinction
vulnerable.example: Koalas are vulnerable due to habitat destruction.

endangered.example: The panda is an endangered species.

resistant.example: Some insects are resistant to pesticides.

disastrous.example: The introduction of cane toads was disastrous for native wildlife.

hedgehog.definition: a small mammal with spines on its back
hedgehog.example: Hedgehogs roll into a ball to protect themselves.

hoglet.example: A litter of hoglets is born twice a year.

quill.definition: a stiff, sharp spine on a hedgehog’s back
quill.example: Hedgehogs use their quills for defense.

forage.example: Hedgehogs forage at night for insects and slugs.

hibernate.example: Some hedgehogs hibernate during cold months.

marsupial.definition: a mammal that carries its young in a pouch
marsupial.example: Kangaroos are marsupials native to Australia.

monotreme.example: The platypus is a monotreme found in Australia.

genome.definition: the complete set of genetic material of an organism
genome.example: Scientists study the hedgehog genome to learn about evolution.

biodiversity.definition: the variety of life in a particular habitat or ecosystem
biodiversity.example: Rainforests are known for their high biodiversity.

puberty.definition: the stage of life when a child's body begins to develop into an adult body
puberty.example: Puberty includes physical and emotional changes as the body matures.

consent.definition: a clear and voluntary agreement to participate in an activity
consent.example: Consent must be freely given and can be withdrawn at any time.

reproductivesystem.definition: the group of organs involved in producing offspring
reproductivesystem.example: The reproductive system works differently in males and females.

menstruation.definition: the monthly shedding of the uterine lining in people with a uterus
menstruation.example: Menstruation is a normal part of the reproductive cycle.

fertilization.definition: the process in which a sperm cell and an egg cell combine
fertilization.example: Fertilization usually occurs in the fallopian tube.

contraception.definition: methods or devices used to prevent pregnancy
contraception.example: Contraception helps individuals plan if and when they want children.

hormones.definition: chemical messengers in the body that regulate growth, mood, and development
hormones.example: Hormones play a major role in the changes experienced during puberty.

sexualhealth.definition: a state of physical, emotional, and social well‑being related to sexuality
sexualhealth.example: Sexual health includes understanding boundaries, consent, and safe practices.

anatomy.definition: the study of the structure of the human body
anatomy.example: Learning anatomy helps students understand how their bodies function.

communication.definition: the exchange of information, feelings, or ideas between people
communication.example: Healthy relationships rely on open and respectful communication.

sibling.example: She has a close relationship with her sibling.

adolescence.definition: the stage between childhood and adulthood
adolescence.example: Adolescence is a period of rapid physical and emotional change.

nurture.definition: to help someone grow and develop
nurture.example: Parents nurture their children by giving them love and support.

interaction.definition: communication or direct involvement with someone
interaction.example: Positive interaction helps children develop social skills.

rewarding.definition: giving a lot of pleasure or satisfaction
rewarding.example: Working with young children can be very rewarding.

milestone.definition: an important stage or event in development
milestone.example: Learning to walk is a major milestone for toddlers.

cognitive.definition: related to thinking, understanding, and learning
cognitive.example: Cognitive skills improve rapidly during early childhood.

selfconscious.example: Teenagers often feel self-conscious about their appearance.

rebellious.example: Many teenagers go through a rebellious phase.

immature.definition: not fully developed emotionally or mentally
immature.example: Some children behave in an immature way compared to their peers.

nurturing.example: A nurturing teacher helps students feel safe and confident.

tantrum.example: Toddlers often throw a tantrum when they are frustrated.

unsteady.example: A baby learning to walk may be unsteady on their feet.

tolerant.example: Good leaders are tolerant of different opinions.

transition.definition: a period of change from one stage to another
transition.example: Moving to high school is a big transition for many students.

growthspurt.definition: a sudden increase in height or size
growthspurt.example: Teenagers often experience a growth spurt during puberty.

clumsy.example: Rapid growth can make teenagers feel clumsy.

abstract.definition: relating to ideas rather than physical objects
abstract.example: Teenagers begin to understand abstract concepts.

memory.example: Her memory for names is excellent.

recall.example: He couldn’t recall the answer during the test.

slipped my mind.example: Sorry, the meeting completely slipped my mind.

nutritious.definition: containing substances needed for good health
nutritious.example: Fruit and vegetables are highly nutritious.

nutrients.definition: substances needed for growth and health

overweight.example: Eating too much fast food can make you overweight.

overeating.example: Skipping meals can lead to overeating later.

ingredients.definition: the items used to make a dish
ingredients.example: Always check the ingredients for allergens.

aerobic.definition: relating to exercise that increases heart and lung activity
aerobic.example: Running is a form of aerobic exercise.

obese.example: A poor diet can lead to becoming obese.

prevent.example: A healthy lifestyle can prevent many illnesses.

allergy.definition: a condition where the body reacts to certain foods or substances
allergy.example: He has a severe nut allergy.

stress.example: Too much stress can affect your sleep.

tension.example: Tension headaches are common during exams.

fulfilment.example: Helping others gives her a sense of fulfilment.

insight.example: The study gave insight into human behaviour.

recreational.definition: related to activities done for enjoyment

personality.definition: the combination of characteristics that form a person’s character
personality.example: Your hobbies can reflect your personality.

optimist.example: An optimist sees challenges as opportunities.

pessimist.example: A pessimist often worries about the future.

realist.definition: someone who accepts situations as they are
realist.example: A realist focuses on practical solutions.

standardofliving.definition: the level of wealth and comfort available to a person
standardofliving.example: The standard of living has improved over time.

qualityoflife.definition: the general well-being of a person
qualityoflife.example: Work-life balance affects quality of life.

coordination.definition: the ability to use different parts of the body together smoothly and efficiently
coordination.example: Good coordination helps children learn to run and jump confidently.

metaphor.definition: a figure of speech that describes one thing by referring to another
metaphor.example: The author uses a river as a metaphor for time.

genre.definition: a category of artistic composition characterized by similarities in form or style
genre.example: Fantasy is her favourite genre of fiction.

narrative.definition: a spoken or written account of connected events; a story
narrative.example: The novel’s narrative shifts between past and present.

symbolism.definition: the use of symbols to represent ideas or qualities
symbolism.example: The colour red is used as symbolism for danger and passion.

aesthetic.definition: concerned with beauty or the appreciation of beauty
aesthetic.example: The painting has a minimalist aesthetic.

protagonist.definition: the main character in a story, play, or novel
protagonist.example: The protagonist faces many challenges throughout the story.

imagery.definition: descriptive language that appeals to the senses
imagery.example: The poet uses vivid imagery to describe the landscape.

manuscript.definition: a handwritten or typed document, especially a writer’s first draft
manuscript.example: The author submitted her manuscript to several publishers.

composition.definition: the arrangement of elements in a work of art or writing
composition.example: The composition of the painting draws the viewer’s eye to the centre.

critique.definition: a detailed analysis and assessment of something, especially a literary or artistic work
critique.example: The critic wrote a thoughtful critique of the film.

satire.definition: the use of humour, irony, or exaggeration to criticize or expose
satire.example: The novel is a satire of political corruption.

allegory.definition: a story, poem, or picture that can be interpreted to reveal a hidden meaning, typically a moral or political one
allegory.example: George Orwell’s 'Animal Farm' is an allegory about the Russian Revolution.

revenue.definition: the total income generated by a business from its activities
revenue.example: The company’s revenue increased significantly last quarter.

liability.definition: a financial obligation or debt owed by a company
liability.example: The business reduced its liabilities by paying off loans early.

equity.definition: the value of ownership in a company after liabilities are deducted
equity.example: Investors received shares representing equity in the company.

inflation.definition: a general increase in prices and a decrease in the purchasing value of money
inflation.example: Inflation caused the cost of groceries to rise.

marketshare.definition: the portion of a market controlled by a particular company or product
marketshare.example: The brand gained market share after launching its new product.

diversification.definition: a strategy of expanding into new products or markets to reduce risk
diversification.example: The company used diversification to protect itself from market changes.

capital.definition: money or assets used to start or grow a business
capital.example: The startup raised enough capital to launch its first product.

profitmargin.definition: the percentage of revenue that becomes profit after expenses
profitmargin.example: Improving efficiency helped increase the company’s profit margin.

entrepreneur.definition: a person who starts and manages a business, taking on financial risks
entrepreneur.example: The entrepreneur launched a successful online clothing brand.

forecasting.definition: the process of predicting future trends based on data
forecasting.example: Sales forecasting helps companies plan for upcoming demand.

merger.definition: the combination of two companies into one
merger.example: The merger created one of the largest companies in the industry.

acquisition.definition: the act of one company buying another
acquisition.example: The acquisition allowed the company to expand its product line.

stakeholder.definition: a person or group with an interest in a company’s success
stakeholder.example: Stakeholders include employees, customers, and investors.

supplychain.definition: the network of suppliers, manufacturers, and distributors involved in producing and delivering a product
supplychain.example: The company improved its supply chain to reduce costs.

outsourcing.definition: the practice of hiring external companies to perform tasks or services
outsourcing.example: Outsourcing customer service can save companies money.

franchise.definition: a business model where a company allows others to operate using its brand and business model in exchange for fees
franchise.example: The fast-food chain expanded through franchising.

bankruptcy.definition: a legal status of a person or company that cannot repay debts
bankruptcy.example: The company filed for bankruptcy after years of financial losses.

investment.definition: the act of putting money into a business or asset with the expectation of profit
investment.example: She made a wise investment in the stock market.

dividend.definition: a portion of a company’s earnings distributed to shareholders
dividend.example: Shareholders received a dividend payment this quarter.

capitalgain.definition: the profit made from selling an asset for more than its purchase price
capitalgain.example: He realized a capital gain when he sold his stocks.

portfolio.definition: a collection of investments owned by an individual or institution
portfolio.example: Diversifying your portfolio can reduce investment risk.

diversify.definition: to spread investments across different assets to reduce risk
diversify.example: Investors diversify their portfolios to protect against market volatility.

bull market.definition: a financial market in which prices are rising or expected to rise
bull market.example: The stock market has been in a bull market for several months.

bear market.definition: a financial market in which prices are falling or expected to fall
bear market.example: Investors are cautious during a bear market.

volatility.definition: the degree of variation in the price of a financial asset over time
volatility.example: High volatility can lead to significant gains or losses in the stock market.

liquidity.definition: the ease with which an asset can be converted into cash
liquidity.example: Stocks are generally more liquid than real estate.

athleticism.definition: the physical qualities that are characteristic of athletes, such as strength, fitness, and agility
athleticism.example: Her athleticism helped her excel in multiple sports.

tournament.definition: a series of contests between competitors to determine an overall winner
tournament.example: The tennis tournament attracted players from around the world.

spectator.definition: a person who watches an event, especially a sports match
spectator.example: Thousands of spectators filled the stadium.

broadcast.definition: a program that is transmitted by radio or television
broadcast.example: The match was broadcast live on national television.

choreography.definition: the sequence of steps and movements in dance or performance
choreography.example: The choreography in the musical was stunning.

entertainmentindustry.definition: the sector of business focused on producing and distributing entertainment
entertainmentindustry.example: The entertainment industry includes film, music, and gaming.

referee.definition: an official who oversees a sports match to ensure rules are followed
referee.example: The referee stopped the game due to a foul.

audience.definition: a group of people who watch or listen to a performance or event
audience.example: The audience applauded loudly after the final act.

performance.definition: an act of presenting a play, concert, or other form of entertainment
performance.example: Her performance in the play received excellent reviews.

sportsmanship.definition: fair and respectful behavior in sports, especially toward opponents
sportsmanship.example: Good sportsmanship is valued more than winning.

athlete.definition: a person who competes in sports or physical activities
athlete.example: The athlete trained hard for the upcoming competition.

coach.definition: a person who trains and instructs athletes or teams
coach.example: The coach developed a new strategy for the team.

championship.definition: a competition to determine the best team or individual in a sport
championship.example: The championship game will be held next week.

ypsilon.definition: the letter Y, often used in mathematics and science
ypsilon.example: In the equation, y represents the dependent variable.

zebra.definition: a wild animal with black and white stripes, native to Africa
zebra.example: Zebras use their stripes for camouflage in the wild.

zenith.definition: the point in the sky directly above an observer
zenith.example: The sun reached its zenith at noon.

zephyr.example: A zephyr cooled the air on the hot summer day.

venous.definition: relating to veins, which carry blood back to the heart
venous.example: The doctor checked the patient’s venous circulation.

vascular.definition: relating to blood vessels in the body
vascular.example: The vascular system is essential for transporting nutrients and oxygen.

vertebrate.example: Humans are vertebrates because we have a spine.

vetiver.definition: a type of grass used in perfumes and for erosion control
vetiver.example: Vetiver has a strong, earthy scent often used in fragrances.

vaccine.definition: a substance used to stimulate the production of antibodies and provide immunity against diseases
vaccine.example: The vaccine helped protect the population from the outbreak.

farcial.definition: relating to or resembling a farce, which is a comedic work using exaggerated and improbable situations
farcial.example: The play was a farcical comedy that had the audience laughing throughout.

fascinating.example: The documentary about space exploration was fascinating.

fascism.definition: a political ideology characterized by authoritarianism and nationalism
fascism.example: Fascism led to the rise of dictators in the 20th century.

fascinate.definition: to attract and hold the attention of someone
fascinate.example: The magician’s performance fascinated the audience.

flavedo.definition: a yellow pigment found in plants
flavedo.example: Flavedo is responsible for the bright colour of citrus fruit peels.

flocculent.definition: having a fluffy or woolly appearance
flocculent.example: The flocculent clouds looked like cotton balls in the sky.

floccule.definition: a small, loosely clumped mass of particles
floccule.example: The floccule formed in the solution during the experiment.

flocculation.definition: the process of particles clumping together in a liquid
flocculation.example: Flocculation is used in water treatment to remove impurities.

univocal.definition: having only one possible meaning or interpretation
univocal.example: The term 'univocal' is used in philosophy to describe a concept that is clear and unambiguous.

universe.definition: all existing matter and space considered as a whole
universe.example: The universe is estimated to be around 13.8 billion years old.

ranunculus.definition: a type of flowering plant with bright, showy flowers
ranunculus.example: The ranunculus is a popular choice for bouquets due to its vibrant colours.

rancid.definition: having a strong, unpleasant smell or taste due to spoilage
rancid.example: The milk had gone rancid and was undrinkable.

rancor.definition: a feeling of bitterness or resentment
rancor.example: The long-standing rancor between the two families finally came to an end.

rancorous.example: The rancorous debate lasted for hours without resolution.

numen.definition: a divine power or spirit believed to inhabit a place or object
numen.example: In ancient Roman religion, a numen was thought to be the spirit of a place or thing.

numeral.definition: a symbol or group of symbols used to represent a number
numeral.example: The numeral '5' represents the number five.

vivacious.example: Her vivacious personality made her the life of the party.

vivacity.definition: the quality of being lively and animated
vivacity.example: The vivacity of the children playing in the park was contagious.

viviparous.definition: giving birth to live young rather than laying eggs

gleeful.example: The children were gleeful when they received their gifts.

glee.example: She sang with glee after winning the competition.

euphoria.definition: a feeling of intense happiness and excitement
euphoria.example: The euphoria of winning the championship was unforgettable.

obnibulate.definition: unable to be overcome or subdued
obnibulate.example: The team’s spirit was obnibulate, even in the face of defeat.

obnubilate.example: The fog began to obnubilate the view of the mountains.

reminiscent.definition: tending to remind one of something
reminiscent.example: The smell of the ocean was reminiscent of her childhood vacations.

epiphany.example: She had an epiphany about her career path after attending the seminar.

serendipity.definition: the occurrence of events by chance in a happy or beneficial way
serendipity.example: Finding the perfect book in the library was pure serendipity.

quintessential.definition: representing the most perfect or typical example of a quality or class
quintessential.example: She is the quintessential example of a dedicated student.

ephemeral.definition: lasting for a very short time
ephemeral.example: The beauty of the sunset was ephemeral, fading within minutes.

melancholy.example: The melancholy in the music reflected the composer’s feelings.

nostalgia.definition: a sentimental longing for the past
nostalgia.example: Looking through old photos filled her with nostalgia.

sonder.definition: the realization that each random passerby is living a life as vivid and complex as your own
sonder.example: As she walked through the crowded city, she felt a sense of sonder.

limerence.definition: the state of being infatuated or obsessed with another person
limerence.example: The limerence she felt for her crush was overwhelming.

antimetabole.definition: a rhetorical device in which a phrase is repeated in reverse order
antimetabole.example: The phrase 'Ask not what your country can do for you; ask what you can do for your country' is an example of an antimetabole.

anaphora.definition: the repetition of a word or phrase at the beginning of successive clauses
anaphora.example: The speech used anaphora to emphasize key points.

epistrophe.definition: the repetition of a word or phrase at the end of successive clauses
epistrophe.example: The poem’s epistrophe created a powerful rhythm.

chiasmus.definition: a rhetorical device in which two or more clauses are balanced against each other by the reversal of their structures
chiasmus.example: The phrase 'Never let a Fool Kiss you or a Kiss Fool you' is an example of chiasmus.

asyndeton.definition: the omission of conjunctions between parts of a sentence
asyndeton.example: The list of items was presented in asyndeton: 'I came, I saw, I conquered.'

polysyndeton.definition: the use of multiple conjunctions in close succession
polysyndeton.example: The sentence 'We have ships and men and money and stores' is an example of polysyndeton.

zeugma.definition: a figure of speech in which a word applies to multiple parts of the sentence
zeugma.example: She broke his car and his heart with one zeugma.

anadiplosis.definition: the repetition of the last word of a clause at the beginning of the next clause
anadiplosis.example: The phrase 'Fear leads to anger. Anger leads to hate. Hate leads to suffering.' is an example of anadiplosis.

epanalepsis.definition: the repetition of the initial part of a clause or sentence at the end of that same clause or sentence
epanalepsis.example: The phrase 'The king is dead, long live the king!' is an example of epanalepsis.

palindrome.definition: a word, phrase, or sequence that reads the same backward as forward

anagram.definition: a word or phrase formed by rearranging the letters of another
anagram.example: The word 'listen' is an anagram of 'silent'.

acrostic.definition: a poem or other composition in which the first letter of each line spells out a word or message
acrostic.example: The acrostic poem spelled out the word 'HOPE' with the first letters of each line.

palimpsest.definition: a manuscript or piece of writing material on which the original writing has been effaced to make room for later writing but of which traces remain
palimpsest.example: The ancient manuscript was a palimpsest, with layers of writing visible beneath the surface.

portmanteau.definition: a word blending the sounds and combining the meanings of two others
portmanteau.example: The word 'brunch' is a portmanteau of 'breakfast' and 'lunch'.

neologism.definition: a newly coined word or expression
neologism.example: The term 'selfie' is a neologism that has become widely used in recent years.

onomatopoeia.definition: the formation of a word from a sound associated with what is named
onomatopoeia.example: The word 'buzz' is an example of onomatopoeia.

ambiguous.definition: open to more than one interpretation; not having one obvious meaning
ambiguous.example: The ambiguous statement left everyone confused about its true meaning.

ambivalence.definition: the state of having mixed feelings or contradictory ideas about something
ambivalence.example: She felt ambivalence about moving to a new city, excited for the opportunity but sad to leave her friends.

ambivalent.definition: having mixed feelings or contradictory ideas about something
ambivalent.example: He was ambivalent about accepting the job offer, torn between the benefits and the drawbacks.

ambit.definition: the scope, extent, or bounds of something
ambit.example: The ambit of the project includes research, development, and marketing.

ambitious.definition: having a strong desire for success or achievement
ambitious.example: She is an ambitious student who aims to become a doctor.

apocrypha.definition: writings or reports not considered genuine or authentic
apocrypha.example: The apocrypha of the ancient text raised questions about its historical accuracy.

apocryphal.definition: of doubtful authenticity, although widely circulated as being true
apocryphal.example: The story about the celebrity’s secret life is likely apocryphal.

apocalypse.definition: the complete final destruction of the world, especially as described in religious texts
apocalypse.example: The novel depicts a post-apocalyptic world after a global catastrophe.

apocalyptic.definition: relating to or resembling an apocalypse; catastrophic
apocalyptic.example: The apocalyptic movie showed the end of civilization as we know it.

rife.example: Corruption is rife in the government, leading to public distrust.

disparate.definition: essentially different in kind; not allowing comparison
disparate.example: The two cultures have disparate traditions and values.

disparity.example: There is a significant disparity in income between the rich and the poor.

euphemism.definition: a mild or indirect word or expression substituted for one considered to be too harsh or blunt
euphemism.example: The term 'passed away' is a euphemism for 'died'.

euphemistic.definition: using or of the nature of a euphemism
euphemistic.example: The euphemistic language in the report softened the impact of the bad news.

connoisseur.definition: an expert judge in matters of taste
connoisseur.example: She is a connoisseur of fine wines and can distinguish between different vintages.

connoisseurship.definition: the expertise and knowledge of a connoisseur
connoisseurship.example: His connoisseurship in art allowed him to identify the authenticity of the painting.

connoisseurly.definition: characteristic of a connoisseur; showing expertise in matters of taste
connoisseurly.example: The connoisseurly review praised the subtle nuances of the dish.

ebony.definition: a dense black hardwood, often used in fine furniture and musical instruments
ebony.example: The piano was made of polished ebony, giving it a sleek and elegant appearance.

ebullient.example: Her ebullient personality made her the life of the party.

iconoclast.definition: a person who attacks or criticizes cherished beliefs or institutions
iconoclast.example: The iconoclast challenged traditional views on art and culture.

iconoclastic.definition: characterized by attacking or criticizing cherished beliefs or institutions
iconoclastic.example: The artist’s iconoclastic work defied conventional norms.

noisome.definition: having an extremely offensive smell; disagreeable or unpleasant
noisome.example: The noisome garbage pile attracted flies and caused a foul stench.

noisomeness.definition: the quality of being noisome; offensiveness or unpleasantness
noisomeness.example: The noisomeness of the polluted river made it unfit for swimming.

earnest.example: She made an earnest effort to improve her grades.

prefigurative.definition: serving as a prefiguration or foreshadowing of something
prefigurative.example: The early scenes of the film were prefigurative of the main plot.

prefigure.example: The novel’s opening chapter prefigures the themes that will be explored later in the story.

herald.definition: a person or thing that announces or signals the approach of something
herald.example: The blooming flowers are a herald of spring.

heraldic.definition: relating to heraldry, the system by which coats of arms and other armorial bearings are devised, described, and regulated
heraldic.example: The heraldic symbols on the shield represented the family’s history and achievements.

heraldry.definition: the system by which coats of arms and other armorial bearings are devised, described, and regulated
heraldry.example: The study of heraldry involves understanding the meanings of various symbols and colors used in coats of arms.

heraldist.definition: a person who studies or practices heraldry
heraldist.example: The heraldist designed a new coat of arms for the noble family.

prosody.definition: the patterns of rhythm and sound used in poetry
prosody.example: The poet’s mastery of prosody contributed to the musical quality of his verses.

winsome.definition: attractive or appealing in a fresh, innocent way
winsome.example: Her winsome smile charmed everyone she met.

wintry.definition: characteristic of winter; cold and bleak
wintry.example: The wintry landscape was covered in snow and ice.

wisteria.definition: a climbing plant with hanging clusters of purple or white flowers
wisteria.example: The wisteria vine covered the trellis with its beautiful blossoms.

zazen.definition: a form of seated meditation in Zen Buddhism
zazen.example: The practitioner sat in zazen to achieve a state of mindfulness and enlightenment.

zealot.definition: a person who is fanatical and uncompromising in pursuit of their religious, political, or other ideals
zealot.example: The zealot was willing to go to great lengths to promote his beliefs, even if it meant alienating others.

atheist.definition: a person who disbelieves or lacks belief in the existence of God or gods
atheist.example: The atheist argued that there is no scientific evidence to support the existence of a higher power.

theist.definition: a person who believes in the existence of a god or gods
theist.example: The theist found comfort in the belief that a higher power was watching over them.

agnostic.definition: a person who believes that nothing is known or can be known about the existence of God or gods
agnostic.example: The agnostic was uncertain about the existence of a higher power and chose to focus on living a meaningful life instead.

deist.definition: a person who believes in the existence of a god or gods based on reason and observation of the natural world, rather than on religious teachings or scriptures
deist.example: The deist believed that the universe was created by a higher power, but that this power did not intervene in human affairs or the natural world.

pantheist.definition: a person who believes that the universe and God are identical, or that God is present in all things
pantheist.example: The pantheist found spirituality in the beauty of nature and the interconnectedness of all living things.

vernacular.definition: the language or dialect spoken by the ordinary people in a particular country or region
vernacular.example: The novel was written in the vernacular, making it accessible to a wide audience.

vernacularize.definition: to translate or adapt something into the vernacular language
vernacularize.example: The Bible was vernacularized into many different languages to reach a broader audience.

nefarious.example: The nefarious plot was uncovered by the detective.

treatise.definition: a written work dealing formally and systematically with a subject
treatise.example: The philosopher’s treatise on ethics is considered a seminal work in the field.

treatises.definition: plural of treatise; written works dealing formally and systematically with a subject
treatises.example: The library contains many treatises on various philosophical topics.

discomfit.definition: to make someone feel uneasy or embarrassed
discomfit.example: The unexpected question discomfited the speaker during the interview.

lexicography.example: The field of lexicography involves researching and documenting the meanings and usage of words.

lexicographer.example: The lexicographer spent years researching the origins and meanings of words for the new edition of the dictionary.

lexicon.definition: the vocabulary of a person, language, or branch of knowledge
lexicon.example: The medical lexicon includes many specialized terms that are not commonly used in everyday language.

elision.definition: the omission of a sound or syllable when speaking
elision.example: In the phrase 'I’m going', the elision of 'am' creates a more casual tone.

elide.definition: to omit a sound or syllable when speaking
elide.example: The poet elided certain words to maintain the rhythm of the verse.

solemnity.definition: the state or quality of being serious and dignified
solemnity.example: The solemnity of the occasion was reflected in the formal attire of the attendees.

solemn.example: The solemn ceremony was attended by many dignitaries.

lyrics.example: The lyrics of the song were heartfelt and resonated with many listeners.

hariolation.definition: a vision or hallucination, especially one that is religious or spiritual in nature
hariolation.example: The saint experienced a hariolation of divine light during meditation.

counterargument.definition: an argument or set of reasons put forward to oppose an idea or theory developed in another argument
counterargument.example: The lawyer presented a counterargument to challenge the prosecution’s case.

counterbalance.definition: a weight that balances another weight
counterbalance.example: The counterbalance on the scale ensured accurate measurements.

counterproductive.definition: having the opposite of the desired effect
counterproductive.example: The new policy was counterproductive, leading to increased confusion and inefficiency.

whilom.example: The whilom king was remembered for his wisdom and justice.

quota.definition: a fixed share of something that a person or group is entitled to receive or is bound to contribute
quota.example: The company set a sales quota for each employee to meet by the end of the quarter.

quotation.definition: a group of words taken from a text or speech and repeated by someone other than the original author or speaker
quotation.example: The essay included several quotations from famous philosophers to support its arguments.

mirth.definition: amusement, especially as expressed in laughter
mirth.example: The comedian’s jokes were met with great mirth from the audience.

denouement.definition: the final part of a play, film, or narrative in which the strands of the plot are drawn together and matters are explained or resolved
denouement.example: The denouement of the mystery novel revealed the identity of the culprit.

catharsis.definition: the process of releasing, and thereby providing relief from, strong or repressed emotions
catharsis.example: The tragic play provided a catharsis for the audience, allowing them to experience and release their emotions.

cathartic.definition: providing psychological relief through the open expression of strong emotions
cathartic.example: Writing in a journal can be a cathartic experience for many people.
